fuzzy_prd: We need a small scheduling library that lets developers rate-limit noisy event handlers without writing their own timer bookkeeping. The core idea: wrap an action or a value, declare a quiet window in milliseconds, and get back a version that only fires or settles after activity has paused for that long. There are two primitives — one for functions/actions that take arguments and run side effects, and one for values that track a frequently-changing source. Both should support leading-edge execution (fire immediately on the first event), trailing-edge (the default, fire after the quiet window), or both together. When both edges are enabled, a burst with only one trigger fires just once; more than one trigger fires on both edges. There must also be a hard upper bound option: if the source keeps firing continuously, execution is forced no later than that bound measured from the first pending trigger. Both primitives need explicit cancel (discard pending) and flush (run immediately) controls. After teardown, any pending action is suppressed — handle disposal the same way the value primitive handles its equality check, where cleanup happens predictably. For the value primitive, a custom equality function can suppress updates when the new value is considered the same as the current one. The library must use a deterministic virtual clock for testing rather than real wall-clock time.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: function useDebounce<T>(value: T, delay: number, options?: UseDebounceOptions): T

trigger_keywords: what happens with single trigger when both edges enabled
leading and trailing single call behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When both leading and trailing edges are enabled, a burst containing only ONE trigger fires exactly ONCE (on the leading edge, no extra trailing execution). A burst with MORE THAN ONE trigger fires twice: once on leading edge, once on trailing edge after the quiet window.

trigger_keywords: what is the default trailing behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The trailing option defaults to true. Without specifying options, the debounced action uses trailing-edge-only behavior: the action fires once after the quiet window fully elapses with no further trigger, using the most recent arguments.

trigger_keywords: how does maxWait interact with continuous triggering
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: maxWait is measured from the first pending trigger of a burst, not from each subsequent trigger. Even if the source keeps firing (resetting the quiet window), execution is forced at maxWait ms after the first trigger. The most recent arguments at that moment are used.

trigger_keywords: what does cancel do after flush
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: cancel discards the pending execution. After cancel, flush does nothing (the cancel already discarded it). After teardown (unmount), any pending execution is suppressed even if time elapses.

The 'alwaysEqual' equality function treats any two values as equal, so no update is ever scheduled regardless of source value changes. The debounced value stays at its initial value permanently. Custom equality is set via options.equalityFn='alwaysEqual'.

Debounced action trace: each actual execution emits 'call#<n> args=<json-array> tag=<marker>' where n is 1-based execution count. observe emits 'calls=<n>' (cumulative count). Debounced value: observe emits 'value=<current>'. The tag reflects the LATEST action version at the moment of firing.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handle disposal the same way the value primitive handles its equality check, where cleanup happens predictably
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The unmount/teardown step in both primitives — after calling unmount, any pending timer is cancelled and further time advancement produces no execution or value update

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please check the start.md specification and the golden reference implementation for details.

feature1_1_trailing.json.description: A debounced function with the default (trailing) behavior collapses a rapid burst of invocations into a single deferred invocation that runs once the quiet period (the configured delay) has fully elapsed without any further invocation. The deferred invocation receives the arguments of the most recent invocation in the burst. No invocation occurs while the quiet period is still running.

feature1_2_leading_only.json.description: When configured for leading-edge-only behavior (invoke on the leading edge, suppress the trailing edge), the function runs immediately on the first invocation of a burst and then suppresses every further invocation that arrives while the quiet period is still active, including the trailing edge. Once the quiet period has elapsed, a subsequent invocation is treated as a new leading edge and runs immediately again.

feature1_3_leading_and_trailing.json.description: When configured to invoke on both the leading and the trailing edge, a burst that contains only a single invocation runs exactly once (on the leading edge, with no extra trailing invocation). A burst that contains more than one invocation runs twice: once immediately on the leading edge and once again on the trailing edge after the quiet period elapses.

feature1_4_burst_then_gap.json.description: With leading-and-trailing behavior, a multi-invocation burst produces a leading invocation and a trailing invocation, and then a further invocation that arrives only after the quiet period has fully elapsed is recognized as a fresh leading edge and runs again. A burst of two invocations followed by one more invocation past the quiet window therefore yields three invocations in total.

cases.expected_output:
call#1 args=[] tag=fn
call#2 args=[] tag=fn
call#3 args=[] tag=fn
calls=3

feature1_5_maxwait.json.description: A maximum-wait bound guarantees that a continuously re-triggered debounced function still runs no later than the configured maximum wait measured from the first invocation of the burst, even though each new invocation would otherwise keep resetting the quiet period and indefinitely postpone execution. When the maximum wait elapses, the function runs once with the arguments of the most recent invocation seen so far.

cases.expected_output:
calls=0
call#1 args=["Right value"] tag=fn
calls=1

feature1_6_cancel.json.description: Cancelling a debounced function discards any invocation that is currently pending so that it never runs, even after the quiet period (and, when configured, the maximum-wait bound) would otherwise have elapsed. After a cancel, advancing time produces no invocation at all.

feature1_7_flush.json.description: An explicit flush operation forces any currently pending invocation to run immediately with its captured arguments instead of waiting for the quiet period. Flushing when nothing is pending does nothing. Flushing after a cancel does nothing, because the cancel already discarded the pending invocation. A pending invocation that is never flushed is also suppressed once the owning component is torn down: tearing the component down and then letting time elapse produces no invocation.

feature1_8_latest_callback.json.description: When the wrapped behavior is replaced between invocations (for example because it closes over state that has since changed), a deferred invocation always uses the most recently provided behavior rather than a stale one. Each emitted invocation line carries a marker identifying which version of the behavior actually ran; the marker reflects the latest version present at the moment the deferred invocation fires, both when the behavior is updated before the invocation is scheduled and when it is updated while an invocation is already pending.

feature2_1_basic.json.description: A debounced value mirrors a source value but only after the source has stopped changing for the configured delay. On the first render the debounced value equals the initial source value immediately. When the source value changes, the debounced value does not update until the quiet period elapses; while it is pending it keeps showing the previous debounced value. If the source changes several times during the quiet period, the debounced value eventually settles on the most recent source value.

feature2_2_leading.json.description: When the debounced value is configured for leading-edge behavior, the very first change to the source value is reflected immediately rather than after the delay. Subsequent changes that occur within the quiet period do not update the debounced value immediately; the debounced value then settles on the most recent source value once the quiet period elapses.

cases.expected_output:
value=Hello
value=Hello world
value=Hello world
value=Hello again, world

feature2_3_cancel.json.description: Cancelling a debounced value discards the pending update so the debounced value remains at its previous settled value even after time elapses. This holds both for an ordinary pending update and for a pending update that is also bounded by a maximum wait: a cancel removes the bounded update as well, so the debounced value never advances to the source value that was in flight.

feature2_4_maxwait.json.description: A maximum-wait bound on a debounced value guarantees the debounced value advances to the latest source value no later than the maximum wait measured from the first pending change, even when the source keeps changing within the quiet period. The maximum-wait timer is only armed once a real change is pending; it is not started on the initial render, so a change introduced after some idle time will not be forced through earlier than the maximum wait measured from that change.

feature2_5_equality.json.description: A debounced value can take a custom equality predicate that decides whether a new source value is considered different from the current one. When the predicate reports that the values are equal, no update is scheduled and the debounced value stays unchanged regardless of how the raw source value differs and regardless of how much time elapses.

feature2_6_revert.json.description: If the source value changes and then changes back to the currently settled debounced value before the quiet period elapses, the debounced value shows no net change once time elapses: the in-flight intermediate value is superseded by the return to the original value, so the debounced value remains at the original value.

feature2_7_flush.json.description: An explicit flush operation on a debounced value applies the pending source value immediately instead of waiting for the quiet period. After a source change leaves an update pending, flushing makes the debounced value jump at once to the latest source value.
